title: Clinidium lalitae
focus: Discovery, characteristics, and ecological role of the newly identified beetle species.
rationale: A specific, localized species discovery with distinct facts such as its location (Talle Valley WLS) and ecological role, suitable for a factual or matching question.
sectionExcerpt: Scientists from the Zoological Survey of India (ZSI) identified a new species of beetle named Clinidium lalitae, in Talle Valley Wildlife Sanctuary, Arunachal Pradesh. It belongs to the subfamily Rhysodinae, a rare group of ground beetles inhibiting forest floors, and plays a significant ecological role in nutrient cycling and soil health.
evidenceBullets: Identified by ZSI in Talle Valley Wildlife Sanctuary, Arunachal Pradesh.
Belongs to subfamily Rhysodinae, a rare group of ground beetles.
Plays a significant ecological role in nutrient cycling and soil health.

title: Lichens
focus: Characteristics, distribution, ecological significance, and recent studies on Lichens.
rationale: Highly examinable for UPSC due to core ecological concepts like symbiosis, bioindicators, and ecological succession, along with specific facts about India's first Lichen Park.
sectionExcerpt: Lichens can survive and remain metabolically active under simulated Mars-like conditions. They cover 8% of land surface, including extreme environments. India hosts over 2,700 species, with its first Lichen Park established in 2020 in Munsiyari, Uttarakhand. Lichens are a symbiotic association between a fungus (mycobiont) and an alga or cyanobacterium (photobiont). They act as bioindicators of air pollution (especially SO2) and are pioneer species in ecological succession, aiding in soil formation.
evidenceBullets: Symbiotic association between fungus (mycobiont) and an alga or cyanobacterium (photobiont).
Bioindicators of air pollution, especially SO2 and heavy metals.
Pioneer species that act as first colonizers in ecological succession and aid in soil formation.
India's first Lichen Park was established in 2020 in Munsiyari, Uttarakhand.
Can survive and remain metabolically active under simulated Mars-like conditions.

title: Tardigrades (Water Bear/Moss Piglet)
focus: Characteristics, survival mechanisms (cryptobiosis), and space experiments involving Tardigrades.
rationale: The unique survival mechanism of cryptobiosis and relevance to space missions make Tardigrades a prime candidate for science and environment questions.
sectionExcerpt: Indian astronaut Shubhanshu Shukla will take Tardigrades to the International Space Station (ISS) for an experiment. Also known as Water Bears or Moss Piglets, these half-millimetre long, eight-legged segmented micro-animals endure extreme temperatures, crushing pressures, and radiation through cryptobiosis. Cryptobiosis is a state of extreme inactivity in response to adverse environmental conditions. Recognized classes include Eutardigrada and Heterotardigrada.
evidenceBullets: Eight-legged segmented micro-animals, also known as Water Bears or Moss Piglets.
Endure extreme hot/cold temperatures, crushing pressures, and radiation through cryptobiosis.
Cryptobiosis is a state of extreme inactivity in response to adverse environmental conditions.
Being taken to the International Space Station (ISS) for experiments by Indian astronaut Shubhanshu Shukla.

title: Cicadas
focus: Habitat, life cycle, and unique characteristics of Cicadas.
rationale: A small concept cluster focusing on their return to Silent Valley NP and their unique sound-producing mechanism, suitable for a single statement-based question.
sectionExcerpt: Cicadas have returned to Kerala's Silent Valley National Park. They occur in warm and temperate biomes and have a life cycle of three stages: egg, nymph, and adult. Adult females usually lay eggs in woody plant tissues. Most species produce an exceptionally loud song by the rapid buckling and unbuckling of drum-like tymbals.
evidenceBullets: Returned to Kerala's Silent Valley National Park.
Life cycle consists of three stages: egg, nymph, and adult.
Produce exceptionally loud songs by the rapid buckling and unbuckling of drum-like tymbals.
Adult females usually lay eggs in woody plant tissues.
